title: What is the difference between ConcurrentHashMap and Collections.synchronizedMap()? When would you use each?

answers.junior: ConcurrentHashMap and Collections.synchronizedMap() both provide thread-safe Map implementations but differ significantly in their locking strategy, performance characteristics, and iteration semantics. Collections.synchronizedMap() wraps any Map with a single intrinsic lock, meaning every read and write operation acquires the same lock on the entire map, which creates a serialization bottleneck under concurrent access.
answers.middle: ConcurrentHashMap and Collections.synchronizedMap() both provide thread-safe Map implementations but differ significantly in their locking strategy, performance characteristics, and iteration semantics. Collections.synchronizedMap() wraps any Map with a single intrinsic lock, meaning every read and write operation acquires the same lock on the entire map, which creates a serialization bottleneck under concurrent access. ConcurrentHashMap, introduced in Java 5 and significantly redesigned in Java 8, uses a much more sophisticated approach: it divides the map into segments (Java 7) or uses lock striping with synchronized blocks on individual buckets (Java 8+), allowing multiple threads to read and write simultaneously without blocking each other in most cases. A critical behavioral difference is that Collections.synchronizedMap() does not make compound operations or iteration atomic. To safely iterate, you must manually synchronize on the map itself: synchronized(syncMap) { for (Map.Entry<K,V> e : syncMap.entrySet()) {...} } — failing to do so risks ConcurrentModificationException.
answers.senior: ConcurrentHashMap and Collections.synchronizedMap() both provide thread-safe Map implementations but differ significantly in their locking strategy, performance characteristics, and iteration semantics. Collections.synchronizedMap() wraps any Map with a single intrinsic lock, meaning every read and write operation acquires the same lock on the entire map, which creates a serialization bottleneck under concurrent access. ConcurrentHashMap, introduced in Java 5 and significantly redesigned in Java 8, uses a much more sophisticated approach: it divides the map into segments (Java 7) or uses lock striping with synchronized blocks on individual buckets (Java 8+), allowing multiple threads to read and write simultaneously without blocking each other in most cases. A critical behavioral difference is that Collections.synchronizedMap() does not make compound operations or iteration atomic. To safely iterate, you must manually synchronize on the map itself: synchronized(syncMap) { for (Map.Entry<K,V> e : syncMap.entrySet()) {...} } — failing to do so risks ConcurrentModificationException. ConcurrentHashMap's iterators are weakly consistent: they reflect the state of the map at some point during or after iterator creation, never throw ConcurrentModificationException, and traversal is safe without external locking. Another behavioral distinction is that ConcurrentHashMap does not permit null keys or values (throws NullPointerException), whereas a synchronized HashMap wrapper does allow nulls. ConcurrentHashMap also provides atomic compound operations unavailable in synchronized maps: putIfAbsent(), computeIfAbsent(), computeIfPresent(), compute(), and merge() — all performed atomically at the key level. For example: cache.computeIfAbsent(key, k -> expensiveOperation(k)) is guaranteed to call the function at most once per key under concurrent access.
answers.tricky: ConcurrentHashMap and Collections.synchronizedMap() both provide thread-safe Map implementations but differ significantly in their locking strategy, performance characteristics, and iteration semantics. Collections.synchronizedMap() wraps any Map with a single intrinsic lock, meaning every read and write operation acquires the same lock on the entire map, which creates a serialization bottleneck under concurrent access. ConcurrentHashMap, introduced in Java 5 and significantly redesigned in Java 8, uses a much more sophisticated approach: it divides the map into segments (Java 7) or uses lock striping with synchronized blocks on individual buckets (Java 8+), allowing multiple threads to read and write simultaneously without blocking each other in most cases. A critical behavioral difference is that Collections.synchronizedMap() does not make compound operations or iteration atomic. To safely iterate, you must manually synchronize on the map itself: synchronized(syncMap) { for (Map.Entry<K,V> e : syncMap.entrySet()) {...} } — failing to do so risks ConcurrentModificationException. ConcurrentHashMap's iterators are weakly consistent: they reflect the state of the map at some point during or after iterator creation, never throw ConcurrentModificationException, and traversal is safe without external locking. Another behavioral distinction is that ConcurrentHashMap does not permit null keys or values (throws NullPointerException), whereas a synchronized HashMap wrapper does allow nulls. ConcurrentHashMap also provides atomic compound operations unavailable in synchronized maps: putIfAbsent(), computeIfAbsent(), computeIfPresent(), compute(), and merge() — all performed atomically at the key level. For example: cache.computeIfAbsent(key, k -> expensiveOperation(k)) is guaranteed to call the function at most once per key under concurrent access. The size() and isEmpty() methods in ConcurrentHashMap are approximate under concurrent modification, returning a best-effort estimate, while the synchronized wrapper always returns an accurate count but at the cost of holding the lock. Use Collections.synchronizedMap() when you need a quick thread-safe wrapper around an existing map, especially when write frequency is low, when you need null key/value support, or when you require strict consistency guarantees. Use ConcurrentHashMap in high-throughput concurrent applications where multiple threads read and write frequently — it can achieve throughput orders of magnitude higher than a fully synchronized map under contention. In production, ConcurrentHashMap is almost always the correct default for shared mutable map state in concurrent applications; Collections.synchronizedMap() is a legacy approach that rarely offers advantages over ConcurrentHashMap in modern Java code.
